channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: microgreen
item.description:
A microgreen is a young edible plant harvested after the cotyledons have expanded and just as the first true leaves begin to appear, typically seven to twenty-one days after planting. Microgreens are grown in shallow trays with a thin layer of growing medium and are cut at the stem just above the soil line.

Microgreens occupy a specific stage in a plant’s life cycle, between sprouts (harvested before cotyledon expansion, eaten whole including the root) and baby greens (harvested after several true leaves have developed). This intermediate stage produces a plant that concentrates flavor compounds, pigments, and nutrients in a small, tender form. Common microgreen varieties include sunflower, radish, broccoli, pea shoots, cilantro, and basil, each with a distinct flavor profile that is often more intense than the mature plant.

item.title: tray
item.description:
A tray is a shallow container used for growing microgreens. In the context of microgreen cultivation, the tray holds the growing medium (typically a thin layer of soil, coconut coir, or hemp mat), retains moisture, and provides the surface area across which seeds are spread for even germination.

The most common tray size is the 10-by-20-inch nursery flat, available in depths from one to two inches. Growing setups often use two trays: a perforated tray that holds the growing medium and a solid tray beneath that serves as a water reservoir for bottom-watering. This arrangement delivers moisture to the roots through capillary action without wetting the leaf surface, which reduces the risk of mold and damping off — a fungal condition that kills seedlings at the stem base.
